narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 72% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid undergraduate grades around a 3.2 GPA, which is right in line with typical applicants, along with middle-family income, full-time study plans, and your background as an Asian female student—none of which stand out as unusual compared to most. However, your school decile rankings paint a different picture: you placed in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades and 4th for Year 3, both notably lower than the average student's 6th decile positions, suggesting challenges in keeping pace academically early on. On top of that, your LSAT score of 23 is significantly below the typical 37, which caught the model's attention right away. When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 decile rank emerged as the single most influential factor, nudging the prediction strongly toward expecting bar failure rather than success. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which had the next biggest pull in the same unfavorable direction. Your Year 1 decile followed closely as the third-most impactful, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Other elements like family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender played smaller roles, each contributing a bit more toward the failure prediction but far less decisively. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, you're below average in those key academic deciles and LSAT, where stronger performers usually cluster in higher ranks and scores above 35. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model highlighting patterns where similar profiles struggled. To boost your future odds, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to aim higher, build stronger study habits to improve grades in any ongoing programs, and consider bar-specific exam strategies since early academic deciles like yours often signal a need for that extra support. With those changes, you could shift your profile toward the successful applicants the model favors. Keep pushing—you've got time to turn this around.
